intro: Claudius Lysias was the Roman military tribune (commander of a cohort of approximately 1,000 soldiers) in Jerusalem who rescued Paul from the Jewish mob in the temple courts (Acts 21:31–40; 22:24; 23:10, 26). When the crowd tried to beat Paul to death, Lysias arrested him, intending to examine him by flogging until Paul revealed his Roman citizenship — at which point the tribune became alarmed, recognizing that he had bound a Roman citizen (Acts 22:25–29). He later transferred Paul secretly to Caesarea Maritima under heavy escort to protect him from a plot to ambush and kill him (Acts 23:12–33), writing a letter to the governor Felix that diplomatically recast events to show Lysias in a favorable light (Acts 23:26–30). His Greek name with a Roman praenomen reflects the common practice of provincial freedmen adopting Roman names upon purchasing citizenship.
